response:
1/8 SOPA tries to solve offshore piracy by making US internet companies police their users - essentially turning Google, Facebook, ISPs into copyright cops. It's like making phone companies responsible for crimes committed over phone calls. 📞

2/8 Here's the thing: a lot of "piracy" happens because content simply isn't available legally. I wanted to watch Downton Abbey S2, found it free on BBC's site... but got blocked for being in the US. So where do people go? Illegal sites.

3/8 The content industry built their business around expensive distribution and complex geographic/time windows. Made sense when distribution was costly. Doesn't make sense in a globally connected internet world.

4/8 They're trying to preserve an outdated licensing model by criminalizing their most passionate fans. Instead of adapting to serve global audiences instantly, they're asking Congress to break the internet's fundamental architecture.

5/8 The internet empowers individuals and creates opportunity. The content industry wants control. These philosophies are fundamentally at odds, and SOPA represents the control side trying to win through legislation.

6/8 DMCA already works well domestically - when companies are notified of copyright violations, they take content down. The real issue isn't the law, it's that content creators won't adapt their distribution to match how people actually consume media today.

7/8 Making YouTube, Facebook, search engines liable for user content will stifle innovation and place massive burdens on services built around user-generated content. We're talking about fundamental changes to how the internet works.

8/8 Bottom line: Instead of criminalizing fans, the industry needs to find ways to serve them inexpensively in the medium they prefer. They can adapt - and they will have to. The alternative is breaking the internet for everyone else.
source: /workspace/pollux/datasets/raw/brad/corpus/transcript-Brad Burnham_ The Right to Piracy_ The Conflict Behind SOPA.mp4_spaced_spaced.txt

response:
1/8 🧵 Everyone thinks SOPA/PIPA is just tech vs content industry - rich guys fighting. But it's actually about YOUR freedom to innovate and use the internet.

2/8 800M people use Facebook. Services you love are at risk. If this passes, you'll pay MORE for things that used to be cheap/free online.

3/8 Here's what's crazy: Foursquare reached 100K users with just $25K and 5-6 people. That's the magic of open source + internet distribution.

4/8 But now? Every startup will need "3 people and a lawyer" from day one. First investor question: "Are you clean? Any liability risk?" 

5/8 The definitions are so vague that NO startup can give crisp answers. Uncertainty = death for innovation and investment.

6/8 Ironic twist: Google (content industry's least favorite) has 200 lawyers. They'll be FINE. But DuckDuckGo with 3 people? Dead in the water.

7/8 This isn't insider baseball. We have massive economic problems ahead. Workers in their 40s need retraining. The internet IS the solution.

8/8 Peer-to-peer education startups cost a fraction of old government programs. We NEED this innovation platform to solve our biggest challenges. 🌐

1/6 🧵 The real victim of SOPA isn't Big Tech - it's the startup that doesn't exist yet.

2/6 Think about it: Google has 200 lawyers on staff. They'll just create a legal team and fight. But what about the next breakthrough company with 3 people?

3/6 We're about to force every startup to hire a lawyer as employee #4. That's not innovation - that's innovation tax.

4/6 The economic transformation ahead means 40-somethings will need to retrain themselves. Government programs are expensive and slow.

5/6 But internet-based peer education? Fraction of the cost, infinitely more accessible. We're talking about killing the very platform we need most.

6/6 This isn't about protecting content. It's about whether we can solve our biggest problems with innovation - or get buried under legal uncertainty. 🚨

1/8 🧵 Government institutions are artifacts of a bygone era - built for bureaucratic hierarchies that dominated the last 150 years. But we're witnessing the emergence of network-based models that organize people far more efficiently.

2/8 Take Uber vs traditional taxi regulation. Instead of hiring inspectors to catch bad behavior, Uber generates real-time data on pickups, drop-offs, driver ratings - all as a natural byproduct of the service. We're regulating with 20th century tools when we have 21st century data.

3/8 The opportunity isn't to let networks run free, but to regulate them using their unique characteristics. Networks like Airbnb create transparency that traditional hotels never could - but we need to figure out privacy vs. accountability.

4/8 Here's what's wild: I see NYC cops using smartphones more than radios. They now have apps with building department data, public housing info - all on consumer devices. We spent decades thinking tech trickled down from NASA. Now it's civilians → military → NASA.

5/8 The consumer market moves faster because there's bigger demand and open source tools let people build quickly. Government is still stuck with procurement laws and contracts that slow everything down.

6/8 But here's the thing - the hard problems aren't technical anymore. They're moral. What's the right balance between individual privacy and government's need to know? These aren't engineering problems, they're questions about the kind of society we want.

7/8 We need to embrace experimentation over perfect solutions. Run lots of small experiments rather than trying to define standards through protracted political processes. Let things emerge organically - that's how we get de facto standards.

8/8 The internet will define how our society is structured going forward. We can't afford boom-bust cycles in government like we had in tech. This is about designing the very structure of our society. It's worth the investment. 🚀

1/6 🧵 Hot take: We're trying to engage citizens in complex policy decisions but dumbing everything down. The challenge isn't technical - it's how do you get "wisdom of crowds" on issues like water policy that involve aquifer science most people can't understand?

2/6 Take water regulation in Canada. There are technical questions about species cycles, aquifer flows that require scientific expertise. Vested interests invest in that science. But how do you let ordinary citizens weigh in without losing nuance?

3/6 The answer isn't to exclude people or oversimplify. It's about reducing complexity to principles that are accessible while preserving the underlying sophistication. We were exploring frameworks for this kind of structured public participation.

4/6 This mirrors what I see in tech: you can't just throw complex systems at users. But you also can't strip away functionality. The magic is in the interface - making sophisticated capabilities feel simple and intuitive.

5/6 Government needs to learn this lesson. Public participation in decision-making doesn't have to be either expert-only or dumbed-down. There's a middle path that respects both complexity and democratic engagement.

6/6 The goal is designing participation frameworks that scale wisdom, not just opinions. That's the kind of innovation government desperately needs. 🗳️

1/8 🧵 I'm the capitalist in the room, but I believe markets fail. The sharing economy has major equity problems because of how it's been capitalized. Blockchain crypto tokens present a totally new way to capitalize networks.

2/8 Bitcoin's genius was bootstrapping a network WITHOUT external capital - just participants. But as mining became expensive, it concentrated in professional operations. The challenge: how do you bootstrap from members without ending up concentrated?

3/8 Right now, we're living through scary consolidation: Google, Apple, Facebook, Amazon dominating because they have more data = better user experiences = more data. It's a self-fulfilling prophecy that's accelerating.

4/8 Here's what's different about blockchains: they're radically open. Public data stores, identical across servers, anyone can join. Protocols are open source. Don't like something? Fork it and take the community with you.

5/8 This matters for AI. We have two choices: AI developed in proprietary data stores of Big Tech, or AI in open public data stores. The right answer? Each individual maintains their own open data store.

6/8 We need to invite algorithms from different providers to operate on OUR data, with authority to decide if we like the output. Otherwise we live in a scary world where proprietary algorithms construct our reality.

7/8 What does blockchain do today? Honestly, very little beyond securing distributed ledgers for Bitcoin storage. But it's mobilized people and capital around open source innovation - and that's huge.

8/8 This isn't a Ponzi scheme because the music never has to stop. Experiments can fail, fork, clone, evolve. We can honor value distribution or not. It's infrastructure for self-organization with distributed value. 🚀
source: /workspace/pollux/datasets/raw/brad/corpus/transcript-Breakout Panel - Next Money_ New Tech for New Finance.m4a_spaced.txt

response:
1/6 🧵 The existential question of our time: Will AI be controlled by Big Tech's proprietary data silos, or will we build it on open, public infrastructure?

2/6 Right now Google/Apple/Facebook/Amazon are winning because more data = better AI = more users = more data. This accelerating cycle is creating dangerous consolidation of power over information and reality itself.

3/6 Blockchain offers a radically different path: open public data stores where anyone can participate, fork protocols, and maintain sovereignty over their own data and algorithmic choices.

4/6 The goal isn't just decentralization - it's personal data sovereignty. Each person should control their own data store and choose which algorithms operate on it. We decide what information shapes our worldview.

5/6 Yes, blockchain today mostly just secures Bitcoin ledgers. But it's already mobilized massive capital and talent around open source innovation. When 90% of startups fail and lose their IP, open source means nothing is truly lost.

6/6 We can fork, clone, evolve, and honor previous value distribution. This creates infrastructure for self-organization that could enable everything from AI co-ops to new forms of economic coordination. The future is open. 🌐
source: /workspace/pollux/datasets/raw/brad/corpus/transcript-Breakout Panel - Next Money_ New Tech for New Finance.m4a_spaced.txt

response:
1/12 🧵 The best entrepreneurs do one of two things: either do the RIGHT thing or do things RIGHT. You can succeed either way, but there's a big difference.

2/12 Entrepreneurs who "do things right" execute flawlessly - they build great software, ship on time, get customers. But they might be solving the wrong problem.

3/12 Entrepreneurs who "do the right thing" might execute messily, but they've found product-market fit. Twitter is the perfect example - did everything wrong operationally, but nailed the core insight.

4/12 This framework completely changed how I evaluate startups. I'd rather back someone riding a rocket ship with messy execution than perfect execution on the wrong idea.

5/12 At USV, we learned this lesson with Multex in the 90s. The founder was incredibly capable - great at building and shipping. But he wasn't solving the right problem.

6/12 It took months of board meetings to help him pivot to what the market actually needed. Once he did (and took full credit for the idea, which was fine), the company went public and sold to Reuters.

7/12 The key insight: as an investor, let founders take credit for pivots. Your job isn't to get recognition - it's to help them get to the right place.

8/12 This is why I always ask: "Are you doing the right thing, or just doing things right?" Both can work, but you need different strategies and timelines for each.

9/12 "Doing things right" entrepreneurs need patient capital and coaching to find their true north. "Doing the right thing" entrepreneurs need operational support to scale without breaking.

10/12 The most dangerous trap is when someone's so good at execution that it masks fundamental product issues. Great metrics can hide a lack of real user need.

11/12 My mentor Rich McGinn taught me this distinction decades ago at AT&T. It's become one of my core frameworks for everything - not just startups, but life decisions.

12/12 Bottom line: Perfect execution on the wrong strategy is still wrong. Messy execution on the right strategy can change the world. Choose wisely. 🚀

1/10 🧵 We almost made one of the biggest mistakes in venture history with Twitter. Here's what we got right, what we got wrong, and the $400B lesson.

2/10 In 2007, we invested in Twitter when VCs were telling them to monetize SMS minutes and make carrier deals. We said: "No, focus on the API, let developers build, stay open and social."

3/10 Twitter actually interviewed US, not the other way around. They spent 90 minutes grilling us on go-to-market strategy. Smart founders choose investors who want to go where they want to go.

4/10 We had deep conviction Twitter was essentially a protocol - an open communication layer for the internet. But we couldn't imagine how to monetize a protocol in 2007.

5/10 The best business model framework we had was "Red Hat for protocols" which wasn't exciting enough. The timing for protocol tokenomics was just 3-4 years too early.

6/10 Then Bill Gross started buying up Twitter clients, accumulating 20-30% of users. This felt existential. The board decided to lock down the platform and kill third-party clients.

7/10 That decision - reactive, defensive - closed off Twitter's potential as an open protocol forever. We went from empowering developers to controlling the experience.

8/10 If we'd had protocol tokens and shared incentive structures, Bill Gross could have accumulated market power WITHOUT creating existential threats. Win-win vs. zero-sum.

9/10 Today, Ethereum is worth ~$450B. Elon bought Twitter for $45B. That's the magnitude of choosing "platform" over "protocol" - a 10x difference in potential value.

10/10 The real tragedy isn't financial - it's what Twitter as an open protocol could have meant for global communication vs. a media megaphone owned by one person. Some opportunities don't come twice. 😞

1/12 🧵 We're living through the most challenging time for consumer startups in decades. Here's why - and what it means for the future of innovation.

2/12 The big four (Google, Apple, Facebook, Amazon) have created unprecedented network effects and consolidation. Their distribution advantages make it nearly impossible for startups to compete once they enter your market.

3/12 I've seen this firsthand - we pioneer markets, technologies, and opportunities, only to watch these giants swoop in with massive advantages. It's not just about copying (though that happens), it's about structural market power.

4/12 Here's the thing: they got there legitimately. But policymakers trying to fix market structure through antitrust? History shows it rarely works. The IBM case was dismissed, Microsoft's dragged on for 10 years with minimal impact.

5/12 What actually dethroned Microsoft wasn't antitrust - it was the internet. Market forces shifted the venue from desktop to web, making Linux dominant on servers. Microsoft couldn't open-source Windows to compete.

6/12 Same with IBM - they created their own disruption by outsourcing PC components, accidentally creating standard interfaces that let anyone build PCs. Market forces, not regulation, changed everything.

7/12 Which brings me to crypto. We're in 1993 internet time - immature infrastructure, but foundational change happening. People ask what crypto can do that databases can't? Today, databases do everything better... except distribute trust.

8/12 And distributing trust is HUGE. It's not just about monetary policy - it's a model for decentralized applications that allow much greater participation at the edges of innovation.

9/12 Remember the mid-90s? 2,500 PC software vendors, 12 different word processors to choose from. By the late 90s? One dominant player in each category. Consolidation killed edge innovation.

10/12 Crypto represents the opposite - a return to the open systems model I've believed in since my Bell Labs days. Open source, decentralized, bottom-up innovation beats closed systems every time.

11/12 We may be heading toward a world where traditional VC returns are impossible in truly decentralized systems. But we're nowhere near that today - there's still massive opportunity in the infrastructure layer.

12/12 The question isn't whether the big four are too powerful (they are). It's whether crypto and decentralized systems can create the next venue shift that makes their advantages irrelevant. I believe they can. 🚀

1/8 🧵 Corporate VC is mostly broken, and here's why from someone who lived it at AT&T Ventures in the 90s.

2/8 The synergy everyone talks about? Nearly impossible to achieve. We tried to get AT&T's network team to test Juniper routers. Cisco gave 60% cash discounts, startups offered warrants instead. Guess who won?

3/8 The problem: executives are measured on EBITDA, not balance sheet appreciation. No way for senior execs to get credit for creating long-term value through strategic investments. Incentives are completely misaligned.

4/8 Most corporate VCs don't give real carry (20% of returns). They pay salaries instead. Imagine hitting a Twitter-sized return - that 20% could be $1B+. Try explaining that comp to the CEO who hired you.

5/8 The corps that do it well? Founder-led companies where leadership understands what building a company actually means. Professional managers rarely get it.

6/8 Here's what worked at AT&T: we were structured as an independent VC partnership with AT&T as sole LP. Real fee structure, real carry, independent investment decisions. Put $350M to work, returned $1.2B.

7/8 But success killed it. New management saw those returns and said 'we should do this ourselves.' Took over in '99 wanting to 'move the needle' with bigger investments. Went long with $1.2B right into the dot-com crash.

8/8 Lesson: if you're going to do corporate VC, structure it like real VC. Independent partnership, aligned incentives, founder-led oversight. Otherwise, you're just playing venture theater. 💸

1/12 🧵 I've been in tech for 30 years, and something fundamental has shifted. We used to build infrastructure - now we invest in networks that ride on that infrastructure. Suddenly, every policy decision in Washington directly impacts innovation.

2/12 At Union Square Ventures, we've seen the extraordinary economics of networks firsthand. Foursquare grew to 100K users on less than $25K. These platforms bootstrap easily, create remarkable efficiencies, and cost almost nothing to promote.

3/12 Here's what blew my mind: When Tumblr's David Karp wanted to test a new feature, he HID it. Just told a few bloggers to mouse over a section. Two weeks later, a kid in Ohio was sharing the "secret" with friends. That's viral distribution at its finest.

4/12 This creates unprecedented opportunity - but also threatens incumbents. For 130 years, our economy was dominated by bureaucratic hierarchies. They're struggling to innovate as fast as decentralized networks can.

5/12 So what do incumbents do? They lobby for regulatory protection. The hotel lobby convinced NYC to ban temporary hotels (targeting Airbnb). Publishers pushed the Research Works Act to slow open access competition. It's creative destruction vs. regulatory capture.

6/12 Every policy proposal should be viewed through this lens: Is this actually protecting consumers, or is it protecting incumbent business models? Usually, it's the latter dressed up as consumer safety.

7/12 The stakes are getting higher. After MegaUpload and JotForm takedowns, every one of our portfolio companies had to reconsider where they store user data. Some are looking at moving DNS servers offshore. We're pushing innovation away from America.

8/12 Other countries see the opportunity. They're creating "internet enterprise zones" with frameworks that protect user data and the freedom to innovate. We could lose our innovation advantage if we're not careful.

9/12 But networks aren't just about economics - they're critical for civil society. Kickstarter will provide more arts funding this year than the National Endowment for the Arts. In the UK, Funding Circle is solving small business lending where banks failed.

10/12 We're going to have to do more with less in the future. Networks can help us efficiently deliver social benefits - from mapping slums in Kenya to empowering mothers in Boston to fight gang violence.

11/12 A woman from Occupy said something that stuck with me: "No one is coming for us." Her generation doesn't expect government or corporations to solve their problems. They grew up in networks - they understand their power intuitively.

12/12 They're not asking us to fix everything. They're asking us not to get in their way as they try to dig themselves out. I think we owe them that. Defend the freedom to innovate. 🚀
source: /workspace/pollux/datasets/raw/brad/corpus/transcript-Brad Burnham's Keynote Speech for CDT's 2012 Annual Dinner.m4a_spaced_spaced.txt

response:
1/8 🧵 The internet has created a new economic model that's turning everything upside down. Networks vs. hierarchies - and policy makers are picking sides, often without realizing it.

2/8 Consider this: Craigslist eliminated call centers, printing presses, trucks, and trees just to help you sell your couch. Airbnb turned spare bedrooms into income streams for hundreds of thousands globally. That's the power of networked efficiency.

3/8 But here's the pattern I'm seeing everywhere: Incumbents can't compete on innovation, so they compete through regulation. Hotel lobbies ban Airbnb. Publishers block open access. Telecom companies burden Skype. Same playbook, different industries.

4/8 The language is always about "consumer safety" but the real goal is protecting existing cost structures. Policymakers have long relationships with these incumbents and few people explaining the innovation side.

5/8 We're at a crossroads. Recent enforcement actions have forced entrepreneurs to consider building outside the US. When our portfolio companies are moving DNS servers offshore, we know something's broken.

6/8 Meanwhile, other countries are creating innovation-friendly zones. The internet is global - innovation will go where it's welcomed. America's advantage isn't guaranteed.

7/8 The kids who grew up in AOL chat rooms and Facebook understand network power intuitively. They're not waiting for us to solve problems - they're asking us not to block their solutions.

8/8 Next time you see legislation about patents, copyright, cybersecurity, or open internet - ask: Does this protect consumers or incumbents? The future of American innovation depends on getting this right. 🇺🇸
source: /workspace/pollux/datasets/raw/brad/corpus/transcript-Brad Burnham's Keynote Speech for CDT's 2012 Annual Dinner.m4a_spaced_spaced.txt

response:
1/12 🧵 The internet as we know it was a complete accident. If AT&T hadn't been deregulated, we wouldn't have the internet today.

2/12 It started with the Carterphone decision → modems → dial-up ISPs → access to what was just a university network. The key? No permission required to get online or put up a website.

3/12 I've lived through both worlds at AT&T Ventures. We invested in Twitter, Tumblr, Foursquare, Etsy. If these founders had to ask permission from a boss, the answer would've been "NO" every time.

4/12 "It doesn't fit our strategy. It compromises our business model. We don't know if there are customers." That's what happens in permissioned environments.

5/12 Compare that to Classic Sports Network (old sports content). We had to give up 18% of the company to John Malone just to get cable distribution. Imagine if every internet startup had to give Comcast equity to launch.

6/12 But here's the problem: that permissionless environment is disappearing. We went from competitive dial-up to broadband duopoly to essentially cable monopoly for advanced speeds.

7/12 Cable companies came from a world of total control - vertically integrated content and user experience. In 2002-2003, they convinced the FCC to classify them as "information services" not "essential telecommunications."

8/12 Now we're seeing the consequences. Comcast tells Netflix: "You're dumping bits on our network, we should get paid." But Comcast IS the terminating access monopoly - the only way to reach their customers.

9/12 This is fundamentally new. There was never a notion that content providers had to pay. You put your server online, anyone could reach it. Netflix only delivers content that subscribers requested.

10/12 The FCC is debating network neutrality rules, but regulatory solutions are problematic. Read 1996-2000 FCC rulings - they had no idea what was coming. Regulations are made with today's knowledge, can't anticipate the future.

11/12 Better solution: create more competition. The same companies opposing regulation are also opposing competition. We need to open these markets, bring competition to the last mile.

12/12 Municipal broadband like what's happening in Chattanooga is the answer. It brings connectivity where there's no competition and creates a more resilient, flexible, dynamic model that preserves innovation. 🚀
source: /workspace/pollux/datasets/raw/brad/corpus/transcript-Envisioning a Gigabit Future_ Featured Remarks of Brad Burnham (Part 6 of 8).m4a_spaced_spaced.txt

response:
1/8 🧵 Most people don't realize the internet exists because of regulatory accidents, not grand design.

2/8 I started at AT&T in 1979, lived through divestiture. Learned the difference between a "service ethic" (AT&T's commitment to universal communication) and an "innovation ethic" (internet's permissionless experimentation).

3/8 The internet's magic came from NO PERMISSION REQUIRED. Dial-up was competitive, nobody controlled content. That's how we got the explosion of innovation we've seen for decades.

4/8 But we're losing this. Cable companies became broadband monopolies and brought their old mindset: "We control what happens on our network." They got the FCC to classify them as information services, not essential utilities.

5/8 Now they're leveraging their "terminating access monopoly" position. Every customer behind their network can ONLY be reached through them. That's unprecedented power they're starting to monetize.

6/8 The Netflix-Comcast fight isn't about network capacity. It's about Comcast realizing they're the gatekeeper to their customers and wanting to extract rent from that position.

7/8 Network neutrality regulations are important but insufficient. Regulatory agencies can't anticipate future innovation. The real solution is competition.

8/8 Municipal broadband is the path forward. It creates competition where none exists, preserves the permissionless innovation that built the internet, and treats connectivity as the essential service it truly is. 💪
source: /workspace/pollux/datasets/raw/brad/corpus/transcript-Envisioning a Gigabit Future_ Featured Remarks of Brad Burnham (Part 6 of 8).m4a_spaced_spaced.txt

response:
1/12 🧵 We're moving from bureaucratic hierarchies to technology-driven networks - and it's bigger than you think.

Bureaucratic structures have only existed for ~150 years. Before that? Flat networks and family organizations. We're going back to our roots, just digitally.

2/12 Media was first because it's purely digital with no friction. But transportation, housing, finance? Governments and incumbents watched what happened to media and said "not in my industry" - so they fought back with regulation.

3/12 Healthcare is next. It's a massive bureaucratic hierarchy where patients aren't getting the care they could for what we spend. Combined with devices recording health data? That's a collision course waiting to happen.

4/12 But here's what's really interesting: Facebook, Google, Amazon - they're the new incumbents. And what undid Microsoft? A shift from desktop to web, and from packaged software to open source.

5/12 The next shift will be about data ownership. If people who CREATE data actually control it, it fundamentally changes network defensibility. Instead of giant platforms, we get smaller companies on open protocols.

6/12 Think about it: Today you can't take your Twitter data to another platform. But with Bitcoin, you can move between wallets seamlessly. People will demand this everywhere - concert tickets, banking, everything.

7/12 The "sharing economy" is misnamed. When NYC studied Airbnb, most units were professionals running businesses, not people sharing spare rooms. It's really the unbundling of traditional industries.

8/12 Here's the problem with today's platforms: Uber takes 27% of every ride and is valued at $47B. To justify that valuation, they'll need to take MORE than 27%. That creates opportunity for someone to come in underneath.

9/12 On AI fears: I believe humans are the culmination of a billion years of evolution. Even if you replicate the circuitry, replicating that is nearly impossible. (Though my partner Albert disagrees - he believes in the singularity!)

10/12 For entrepreneurs: Don't try to find customers for your product. Find product-market fit first. I see too many companies pivot from consumer to enterprise with the same product when nobody uses it. That's denial.

11/12 On privacy vs collective intelligence: We might be giving up amazing possibilities. Our portfolio company Duolingo can shrink a 2-hour language test to 20 minutes by analyzing every click. Imagine what we could solve with shared health data.

12/12 The key question: How do we get the benefits of collective intelligence while maintaining personal autonomy over our data? That's the problem everyone in tech should be working on. 🧵
source: /workspace/pollux/datasets/raw/brad/corpus/transcript-#TOA Interview with Fred Wilson & Brad Burnham of Union Square Ventures.m4a_spaced_spaced.txt

response:
1/8 🧵 Why I think corporate VCs largely suck (and what they should do instead):

If you think a company is strategic and important to your business, just buy it. If not, don't invest. Corporate investing doesn't really do anything for the corporation.

2/8 Here's what governments should do for startup ecosystems: "Transportation, schools, parks, police, fire." Make it a great place to live, and everything else takes care of itself.

Don't pick winners. Don't create complicated financial structures.

3/8 40% of European VC funding comes from state-backed sources. That's a market failure. The capital exists in Europe - it should be going to work without government filling gaps.

4/8 Berlin vs London for startups:

Berlin: Grassroots, gritty, tech-heavy, lower cost, younger creative talent pool. Great for building something completely new.

London: More established, expensive, better for financial services/media due to existing talent.

5/8 What Berlin needs most: Young, technically savvy VCs who control their own deals. Too many scouts take deals back to firms asking "where's the revenue?" - those deals die.

6/8 On capitalism and ethics: I think capitalism is evolving in the network economy. You can't be evil and expect people to affiliate with your network and create value for you. The system is self-correcting.

7/8 But here's the hard part for CEOs: If an employee violates your values and you don't fire them, you become complicit. Even if they're a good person who made one mistake. Not enforcing values weakens them for everyone.

8/8 We're very open with our ideas at USV. Our LPs ask "why are you giving away proprietary assets?" Because there are always more smart people outside the room than inside. Being in the flow of ideas matters more than controlling them. 🧵
source: /workspace/pollux/datasets/raw/brad/corpus/transcript-#TOA Interview with Fred Wilson & Brad Burnham of Union Square Ventures.m4a_spaced_spaced.txt

response:
1/12 🧵 After 20+ years in VC, I've learned that the biggest threat to innovation isn't technical limitations—it's consolidation by big tech platforms.

2/12 We saw this movie with Web2: Google, Apple, Facebook, Amazon consolidated power. Now with AI requiring billions to train models, we're seeing the same pattern emerge.

3/12 The question isn't whether AI will be transformative—it's whether it will be open or controlled by a few giants.

4/12 What excites me: AI might actually break down the silos that big tech has built. When users can tell an AI "copy my SoundCloud playlist to Spotify," those walls start crumbling.

5/12 I'm betting on "can't be evil" infrastructure vs "promises not to be evil." Web3 showed us tech that structurally prevents rug pulls. We need the same for AI.

6/12 The pattern is clear: massive infrastructure investments (internet, social platforms, blockchains, now AI models) create the foundation for the next wave of innovation.

7/12 But here's what's different this time: AI democratizes building. Someone built an app over the weekend without writing code. That's the Web2 AWS moment for AI.

8/12 The real opportunity is empowering individuals with AI briefing books—personalized, contextual information organization that breaks free from functional silos.

9/12 Voice interfaces will be the unlock. Not because the tech is new, but because intelligent agents can finally DO things, not just convert speech to text.

10/12 Early adopters won't be us boomers. Just like tween girls adopted TikTok first (no prior UI commitments), new interfaces will emerge where people have no legacy habits.

11/12 After decades of investing, what keeps me here? Sitting across from entrepreneurs who've thought deeply about problems. It's the fastest way to learn—pure interrogation until that "aha" moment.

12/12 The next few years will determine whether we get structural openness or repeat the consolidation cycle. I'm optimizing for the former. 🚀

1/8 🧵 Confession: I passed on Airbnb because I was "too old to sleep on an air mattress." Classic mistake—I thought I was the market.

2/8 Also missed Square and Stripe. Saw smart founders tackling regulated industries but didn't grasp that DEVELOPERS were the real customers, not traditional financial services users.

3/8 The pattern of my misses? Underestimating new customer bases and distribution models that bypass traditional gatekeepers.

4/8 But here's what we got right early: seeing Bitcoin as a protocol, not just "internet money." That insight unlocked everything—storage protocols (Filecoin), identity protocols (Stacks).

5/8 The "permissionless network" pattern recognition hit me immediately. Felt like the early internet all over again. Raw pattern recognition beats complex analysis.

6/8 Biggest learning: venture economics changed with the web. Tumblr taught us—20M users, 2 people. Shared infrastructure means you don't need massive upfront capital.

7/8 That's why we developed new muscles: writing smaller initial checks, adding capital as companies grow organically rather than front-loading huge rounds.

8/8 The lesson: be willing to change your model when the underlying economics shift. What worked in enterprise software doesn't work for internet-native businesses. 📊

1/12 🧵 We're witnessing a fundamental shift that will be as transformative as relational databases were in the 1970s. Blockchain isn't just about efficiency - it's about disaggregating market power and re-decentralizing the web.

2/12 Think about pre-internet media: TV stations, newspapers controlled distribution and therefore what consumers saw. Then TCP/IP let anyone reach anyone else directly. Distribution bottlenecks disappeared overnight.

3/12 Today we face new consolidation. When was the last major consumer web service to reach global scale? Snapchat - over 5 years ago. There's a reason Twitter shut down Vine and companies can't compete with Facebook Live: network effects.

4/12 The network effect creating market power for Google, Apple, Facebook, Amazon comes from having consumer data concentrated in single databases. They replace the central switches telecom companies once controlled.

5/12 Here's the profound change: blockchain enables systems where data is distributed among multiple people, no longer concentrated with single vendors. This could allow for true re-decentralization of the web.

6/12 Imagine inverting today's model: instead of consumers going to 20-30 sites each with incomplete views of them, consumers control comprehensive data stores and 20-30 sites come to THEM on a permissioned basis.

7/12 This fundamentally undermines network effect market power. You wouldn't need Facebook-scale to provide useful services. A single permission from a consumer to access their comprehensive data store could enable powerful new services.

8/12 There's something unprecedented happening: open source now has its own business model through tokens. If you create a blockchain system with tokens, as usage grows, token value grows due to limited supply and transaction necessity.

9/12 This creates a fascinating tension: for company equity to be valuable, the company must retain significant tokens. But for the network to succeed, tokens can't be hoarded. The tokens might become more valuable than the equity.

10/12 I see two opportunities: efficiency gains in existing businesses through private blockchains (very real value), and the more disruptive potential of public blockchains enabling market disaggregation.

11/12 Financial institutions lunch executive told me something striking: normally they'd consolidate back offices now due to regulatory pressure, but Elizabeth Warren won't let them merge. Solution? Shared databases without consolidating front offices.

12/12 We're moving toward an inverted pyramid: few large, robust, well-tested blockchains at the base, with services built on top rather than spinning up new blockchains. The future is about choosing the right layer to build on. 🚀
source: /workspace/pollux/datasets/raw/brad/corpus/transcript-Panel Discussion_ _Blockchain and FOSS, Problems and Possibilities_ (SFLC 2016).m4a_spaced1.txt

response:
1/12 🧵 The most dangerous thing about dominant platforms isn't their size—it's their ability to watch innovation happen on top of them, then decide what to internalize.

I've seen this movie too many times. Twitter, Facebook, Microsoft—they all follow the same playbook.

2/12 Early Twitter was an open API paradise. Tweetie, TweetDeck—brilliant apps built on top of Twitter's data. Twitter didn't even have their own mobile apps!

Then advertising became the business model. Suddenly they needed to control how content was displayed.

3/12 What happened next? They cut off all those value-adding developers. Same story with Zynga on Facebook—even when founders had personal relationships with Zuckerberg.

It's an incredible luxury as a platform: watch others innovate, then absorb what works.

4/12 This is why 'trustlessness' matters—not about trusting governments, but about trusting platforms.

No smart developer builds on closed platforms anymore. They've been burned too many times. We need platforms that literally CAN'T be evil.

5/12 The ICO bubble was naive enthusiasm meeting real frustration. Developers who'd been screwed by platforms saw crypto as salvation.

But we had tiny teams with huge war chests trying to build on infrastructure that couldn't support them at scale.

6/12 It was like the 1999 IPOs happening in 1993—before we even had browsers. Companies raised $100M when the deployment platform wasn't ready.

Now some well-funded crypto projects are actually investing in infrastructure. That's smart.

7/12 Think about Uber's economics: drivers, passengers, shareholders splitting the pie. If shareholders demand 3x returns on a $70B valuation, that's massive rent extraction.

This opens the door to skinnier protocols with cryptocurrency incentives.

8/12 Imagine ride-sharing where drivers and passengers own tokens that appreciate as the network grows. The value creators actually participate in the upside—that doesn't happen in shareholder models.

It's not about replacing capitalism, it's about better aligning incentives.

9/12 Healthcare is one of the least efficient markets on Earth. A generation raised on the internet expects Uber-like experiences but gets 'call for an appointment in 3 weeks.'

The edges are where innovation happens first in regulated markets.

10/12 On venture capital: We keep our funds small ($150-200M) and use a paired model—core fund for outsized returns, opportunity fund for stable returns with lower fees.

It's about being respectful of LP money, not just raising the biggest fund possible.

11/12 The consolidation we're seeing isn't just about market dominance—it's killing innovation. When platforms can absorb or kill any innovation built on top of them, entrepreneurs stop building.

We need protocols that preserve the ability to innovate.

12/12 I'm not at conviction yet on crypto—still in the intuition phase. But when I look at the alternative (continued platform consolidation killing innovation), the direction becomes clearer.

The re-decentralization of the web isn't just possible—it's necessary.

1/8 🧵 Hot take: The venture capital business is cannibalizing itself, and that's exactly how it should be.

Innovation always finds efficiencies and enables lower prices. Our job is to be on the front end of that disruption, not the back end.

2/8 Look at Discord going 90-10 on game revenue splits while others take 30%. "Your margin is my opportunity" in action.

If we're backing the companies creating these efficiencies early, we can deliver great returns while improving markets.

3/8 Secondary transactions for founders: I'm not ideological either way, but there's a right way and wrong way.

$2-4M to take pressure off? Smart. $10-20M? Now you're thinking about planes and second homes. That's a distraction.

4/8 We learned this lesson with Delicious. Josh Schachter sold to Yahoo for ~$25M when we could have offered a smaller secondary to solve his near-term needs.

Knowing what we know now, we would have kept him building longer.

5/8 The embarrassing thing about VC: extraordinary returns create a pool of capital that comes to market AFTER the puck has moved.

Web 2.0 returns funded bids for consumer services competing against dominant incumbents. That's tough math.

6/8 Even if you think you're outside Facebook's 'kill zone,' don't be shocked if they launch financial services. They have the platform, reach, and ability to deliver that product.

Focus is what usually stops them, but 'connecting people with services' isn't off-brand.

7/8 We sell shares back to LPs when companies go public and get off boards. Could we make more money holding longer? Sure.

But our job is working with entrepreneurs early. There's a point where we add less value—that's when we step aside.

8/8 The worst thing entrepreneurs can do? Email all five partners at once.

Everyone assumes someone else will respond. Figure out which partner works on your space, get a warm intro from a portfolio company. Much better odds.

1/12 🧵 We're at a fascinating inflection point with blockchain tech. The real challenge isn't technical - it's governance. When quantum computing breaks current encryption, how do we migrate entire blockchains? Who decides? This is where things get interesting...

2/12 Look at what happened with Ethereum's DAO hack. The community forked the entire blockchain, essentially creating two versions: Ethereum (~$1B market cap) and Ethereum Classic (~$100M). People literally voted with their wallets on governance models.

3/12 This reveals something profound: we're not just building new tech, we're experimenting with new forms of collective decision-making. Democracy? Benevolent dictators like Linus Torvalds? Something in between?

4/12 Here's my TCP/IP analogy: Just like the internet moved us from centralized telecom to distributed protocols, blockchain could do the same for data. Instead of network effects concentrating in Facebook/Google silos, we get protocol-level competition.

5/12 But there's a key difference. TCP/IP was beautifully simple: send/receive. Blockchain is more complex - we're trying to encode social mechanisms into protocols. That's harder than routing packets.

6/12 The opportunity is massive though. As Jason Furman noted, market concentration is the biggest drag on US growth. Blockchain offers a path to genuine decentralization - not just in tech, but in economic power structures.

7/12 Think about it: what if network effects worked FOR competition instead of against it? What if your data portability was built into the protocol layer, not dependent on corporate goodwill?

8/12 The trick is solving the right problem at the right layer. TCP/IP succeeded because it just handled routing - everything else was built on top. For blockchain, maybe we focus on the data layer: store/don't store. Let applications figure out the rest.

9/12 We need to resist the urge to solve everything at once. Start with fundamental data portability and ownership. Build simple, robust primitives. Let innovation happen on top.

10/12 The regulatory challenge will be convincing policymakers that this "scary, anarchist, unregulated" technology is actually their friend. It's a tool for breaking up concentrated power, not consolidating it.

11/12 But we have to get the governance right. These systems need to evolve over decades. They need to be community-owned, not controlled by any single entity. As Bill Joy said: there are always more smart people working elsewhere.

12/12 We're not just building new databases. We're potentially restructuring how economic value flows in society. That's why getting the fundamentals right matters so much. The stakes couldn't be higher.
source: /workspace/pollux/datasets/raw/brad/corpus/transcript-Panel Discussion_ _Blockchain and FOSS, Problems and Possibilities_ (SFLC 2016).m4a_spaced2.txt

response:
1/12 🧵 The internet's biggest threat isn't hackers or spam - it's legacy industries using regulation as a weapon against innovation.

I've watched this playbook unfold: incumbents write laws, politicians who "still have secretaries print emails" pass them, startups get crushed.

2/12 Here's what's wild: When NYC debated banning "temporary hotels," the hotel lobby never even mentioned Airbnb by name. They talked about "unscrupulous landlords" and "unsavory people."

The city council had never heard of Airbnb. But the hotel industry knew exactly what they were doing.

3/12 This is economic self-sabotage. We're not preventing disruption - we're making it MORE violent.

It's like grass growing through pavement. You can slow it down, but when it finally breaks through, the explosion will be catastrophic instead of graceful.

4/12 Network companies like Airbnb, Uber, Lending Club aren't just apps - they're dismantling 200 years of bureaucratic hierarchy by connecting creators directly with consumers.

No middleman bank. No dispatcher. No hotel chain. Just pure efficiency.

5/12 The economics are stunning: Foursquare hit 100K users on $25K. Tumblr was in 100 countries with 12 people.

This wasn't possible 15 years ago. The internet's layered architecture + permissionless innovation = the biggest creativity explosion in centuries.

6/12 But here's the problem: cyber-utopians think "let the internet run free" solves everything. Wrong.

Unscrupulous hackers in Russia can steal from your mom. Bad actors exist on every platform. We NEED regulation - just not the dinosaur version we have now.

7/12 I call it Regulation 2.0: Stop treating the internet as the PROBLEM. Start treating it as the SOLUTION.

Uber has more transportation data than any regulator has ever seen. But they won't share it if you're using it to destroy them.

8/12 Current model: Inspectors in unmarked cars chase taxis around NYC.

Future model: Regulate the EDGES of networks, not the middle. Give platforms liability protection in exchange for data transparency.

This isn't rocket science - it's common sense.

9/12 The startup community has proven we can STOP bad legislation (remember SOPA?). But we've never proven we can BUILD good policy.

Destruction is easy. Construction is hard. We need to get politically engaged, not just defensively reactive.

10/12 Iceland could lead this revolution. Small, homogeneous population. Tractable political process. Natural entrepreneurial culture.

Imagine becoming the first country with internet-native regulation. The global brand value would be immense.

11/12 This isn't about left vs right politics. It's about future vs past.

Whichever party seizes the "future" vision will capture young people who grew up inside Facebook and understand network effects. That's a powerful political base.

12/12 The choice is simple: Graceful evolution or violent disruption.

Either we build Regulation 2.0 that works WITH networks, or we wait for the incumbents to collapse under their own regulatory weight.

I know which future I'm betting on. 🚀

1/12 🧵 The problem with Uber isn't just bad management - it's the equity model itself. At a $60-70B valuation, they need $10B+ in earnings. That means extracting MORE value from drivers and passengers over time to satisfy shareholders.

2/12 But here's the thing: Uber's core innovation - GPS coordination, mapping, reputation systems - hasn't changed much since launch. What if we could reduce this to a protocol instead of a company?

3/12 Imagine a token-based protocol where drivers and passengers own the network. As more people join, the token appreciates in THEIR wallets - not some distant shareholders. Participants capture the value they create.

4/12 This isn't just theory. We're moving toward "networks of networks" - smaller communities with their own currencies that can seamlessly exchange. Think automatic currency conversion behind the scenes.

5/12 The real power? Your reputation becomes portable and verifiable across networks. In my small 250-person community, everyone knows everyone. Your word is your bond. Blockchain lets you build that same trust at scale.

6/12 Yes, putting your entire interaction history on an immutable ledger sounds terrifying. But if YOU control the keys and decide what to reveal for each interaction, it becomes a powerful tool for building trust.

7/12 The energy problem? Proof of work was like HTTP 1.0 - so simple that it worked despite being "stupid." We're moving to proof of stake and fractal chain architectures that scale like nature does.

8/12 Here's what keeps me up: technology is morally agnostic. I was naive about the early internet - thought open communication would end dictatorships. Dictators just got better at using the tools.

9/12 This time we know better. China's social credit system, India's cashless society - blockchain makes surveillance easier too. We need to build competing systems FAST.

10/12 The advantage? The cycle from Web 2.0 to Web 3.0 is short enough that we remember getting "hoodwinked" last time. We can learn from those mistakes.

11/12 Tokens drive attention because capital drives creative output. Within our current system, that's how we can fund alternatives to extractive platforms.

12/12 Bottom line: we can either let others use these tools against us, or we can use them to build the cooperative, decentralized future we want. The choice is ours. 🚀

1/12 The internet's layered architecture is accidentally revolutionary. Engineers separated communications from applications without realizing they'd create the biggest explosion of creativity in 200+ years.

2/12 This separation means any entrepreneur can reach Bangladesh without building infrastructure. What took corporations massive resources now costs almost nothing - Foursquare hit 100K users on $25K, Tumblr reached 100 countries with 12 people.

3/12 Network companies like Airbnb and Uber aren't providing services - they're cutting out 200 years of bureaucratic hierarchy by connecting creators directly with consumers. This is fundamentally transforming how we create economic value.

4/12 These networks make us radically more efficient with existing resources. Someone driving Hamburg to Dusseldorf anyway can take passengers. Spare bedrooms become hotels. We're optimizing assets that were previously underutilized.

5/12 But here's the problem: incumbents write the regulations that constrain their disruptors. NYC's hotel lobby literally wrote the law that now fines Airbnb hosts - and the city council had never even heard of Airbnb when they passed it.

6/12 This creates a dangerous dynamic. Blocking efficient networks doesn't stop them - it's like grass growing through pavement. But it makes the eventual disruption much more violent instead of gradual and manageable.

7/12 We need "Regulation 2.0" - treating the internet as the solution, not the problem. An Uber ride generates more regulatory data than any taxi inspector has ever seen: pickup, dropoff, route, time, payment, mutual ratings.

8/12 Instead of forcing networks into old regulatory boxes, we should regulate the edges, not the middle. Give networks liability protection in exchange for data sharing. This creates better oversight with lower costs.

9/12 The startup community has proven we can stop bad legislation (remember SOPA/PIPA?) but we haven't shown we can advocate FOR good policy. That's the harder challenge we must solve.

10/12 This isn't about left vs right politics - it's about future vs past. The party that seizes the internet-native vision will capture young voters who grew up inside Facebook and understand the platform's value.

11/12 Iceland has a unique opportunity here. Small, homogeneous population with tractable politics could pioneer internet-friendly regulation and build a global brand as the place that works WITH networks, not against them.

12/12 The question isn't whether these hyper-efficient networks will win - they will. The question is whether we'll have graceful creative destruction or violent disruption. The choice is in how we regulate today.
